title: Accuracy vs. Efficiency Tradeoffs
concept: Choosing a CNN is a tradeoff problem: the best model depends on the deployment hardware and product constraints, because accuracy, latency, memory, and energy all matter together.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what architectural trick made MobileNet famous for mobile use?

options.text: Replacing many standard convolutions with depthwise-separable ones
Removing all feature maps after each layer

exercises.explain: Right — now let's talk about the bigger decision: when is that tradeoff actually worth it?

cards.title: A race car is bad in a traffic jam
cards.body: The fastest race car on an open track is not the best vehicle for a crowded city street. CNNs are similar: a model that wins on a powerful benchmark machine may be the wrong choice on a battery-limited camera or a low-cost robot.

cards.title: What is an accuracy-efficiency tradeoff?
cards.body: An accuracy-efficiency tradeoff means improving one dimension can make another worse. A larger model may gain accuracy but cost more latency, memory, and power. A smaller model may run beautifully on-device but lose some recognition quality. Model selection is about finding the best balance for the deployment target.

cards.title: The best tool depends on the trip
cards.body: For a day hike, you want a light backpack. For moving into a house, you want a truck. Asking 'Which is better?' without context misses the point. Asking 'Better for what environment?' is the right question — and that is how CNN tradeoffs work too.

cards.title: Same task, different winners
cards.body: A warehouse robot, a cloud photo service, and a smartwatch might all need image recognition. The cloud service can afford a larger, heavier model. The robot may need very low latency for safety. The watch may care most about battery and memory. The same benchmark table can produce three different best choices.

cards.title: There is no universal champion
cards.body: People often ask for the single 'best' architecture. Usually there isn't one. There are only models that are best under a particular budget: milliseconds, megabytes, watts, and acceptable error rate. That is why efficient CNN design became such an important research direction.

cards.title: Leaderboard thinking vs. deployment thinking
cards.body: A model choice changes when you move from benchmarks to real hardware.

left.text:
Ask: highest accuracy?
Ignore latency and memory
One score dominates

right.text:
Ask: fits the device?
Latency, memory, energy all count
Best model depends on context

exercises.prompt: Why might two engineers choose different CNNs for the same image task?

options.text: Because they may be deploying to different hardware with different latency, memory, or power budgets
Because CNN architecture has no effect on runtime
Because accurate models cannot run outside a data center
Because benchmarks always rank models randomly

exercises.explain: Correct — deployment context changes the objective.

exercises.prompt: True or false: if Model A has the highest top-1 accuracy on a benchmark, it must also be the best model for a smartwatch app.
exercises.explain: False — the watch may not have the memory or energy budget for that model.

exercises.sentence: In deployment, the 'best' CNN is the one that fits the hardware ___, not just the one with the top score.
exercises.explain: Exactly — architecture choice is always tied to the environment.

exercises.prompt: Which statement shows good deployment thinking?

options.text: For a drone, I should compare accuracy together with latency, memory use, and energy cost
I only need the model with the top benchmark rank, even if it misses the timing budget
Hardware never matters once a model is trained

exercises.explain: Yes — deployment thinking evaluates the full operating budget.

exercises.prompt: You must choose a CNN for a battery-powered inspection drone. Model X is more accurate but too slow for the drone's control loop. Model Y is slightly less accurate but meets the timing and power budget. Which is the better engineering choice?

options.text: Model Y, because a model that misses the hardware budget can be worse in practice even if its raw accuracy is higher
Model X, because raw benchmark accuracy should override latency and energy limits
Neither, because drones cannot use CNNs for vision
Model X, because slower models are always safer

exercises.explain: Exactly. The right model is the one that solves the real deployment problem, not the one that looks best in isolation. Next: zoom out and recap how CNN design evolved from brute-force scaling toward smarter efficiency.
